result:
The first time I realized that time was leaking out of me like water through cupped hands, I was standing in line at a grocery store, holding a single onion. The fluorescent lights hummed overhead like a swarm of mechanical bees, and the woman in front of me was arguing with the teenage cashier about a coupon that had expired three days earlier. She kept saying, “But I bought it on Tuesday,” as if time itself could be negotiated like a used car. I looked down at the onion—its papery skin the color of old parchment—and felt this sudden, vertiginous lurch, like the floor had tilted. Somewhere between the produce aisle and the checkout conveyor, I had lost an entire decade. Not metaphorically. Literally. Ten years, gone. I could feel them missing, the way you can feel a tooth that’s been pulled, an ache in the architecture of your mouth.

I paid for the onion with cash I didn’t remember earning and walked out into the parking lot where the sky was the exact shade of a bruise. I sat in my car—the key fob was in my pocket, though I couldn’t recall driving here—and peeled back the onion’s skin. Layer after layer, expecting to find something at the center: a pearl, a key, a tiny folded map. But there was nothing, just the tight white core, bitter and eye-watering. I ate it raw, standing there next to a cart return, because I needed to taste something that belonged to the earth and not to the fluorescent kingdom of expiration dates.

When I got home, the house was full of strangers wearing my memories like costumes. A man in the kitchen making coffee called me “babe” and kissed my cheek with the casual intimacy of a long partner. He smelled like cedar and dish soap. I let him. I drank the coffee he handed me—two sugars, no cream—and nodded when he mentioned the roof needing shingles. There were photos on the mantle I didn’t remember taking: us on a ferry, wind whipping my hair into his mouth; us at a farmers market holding a gnarled squash like a trophy; us older, softer, standing beside a girl with gap teeth and my crooked smile. I carried the onion skin in my coat pocket, brittle as moth wings, and when no one was looking, I slipped it between the pages of a book on the shelf—something about bird migrations—because I needed proof that I had existed alone, unattached, unwitnessed.

At night I lie awake listening to the house settle, the way beams sigh after years of holding up lives. The man—Mark, I’ve learned—sleeps with one arm flung across me like I’m a life raft. I stare at the ceiling and try to reverse-engineer love. If I trace the constellation of freckles on his shoulder, will I feel the gravity that drew them into orbit around me? If I whisper “I love you” into his sleeping ear, will the words ripen into truth, the way bananas release ethylene and sweeten everything nearby? I practice in the dark, shaping my mouth around the vowels until they lose meaning and become just sound, a small animal in the sheets.

Sometimes the girl—our daughter—pads in smelling of toothpaste and pillow warmth. She crawls between us, a hot kernel of trust, and tells me about her dreams: a library where books grow on trees, a whale who sings in her closet. I listen like an anthropologist, cataloging the tiny artifacts of a childhood I supposedly co-authored. Once she asks why I cry when I chop onions. I tell her it’s because onions remember the ground they came from, and memory is sulfurous, sharp. She accepts this with the serene authority of small people who still believe mothers are omniscient. I don’t tell her that I cry because I’m mourning versions of her I’ve never met—the infant, the toddler, the nine-year-old who learned to ride a bike while I was somewhere else, decade shopping.

I start collecting lost things. Not in a conscious way at first; it begins as coincidence. A lone mitten frozen to a chain-link fence. A child’s tooth in the parking lot of a roller rink. A love letter in the gutter, rain smearing ink into bruise-colored clouds. I bring them home in coat pockets, grocery bags, the hollow of my cheek. I arrange them on the windowsill like museum specimens. Mark jokes that I’m building a shrine to entropy. The girl calls it the “ouch museum.” She adds her own contraband: a cracked marble, a sticker with the face half-scratched off, a purple sequin that twirls when you breathe on it. Each object vibrates with the hum of having been dropped, forgotten, let go. I develop a private taxonomy: things slipped versus things thrown, things mourned versus things replaced. At night I whisper to them, “I see you. I keep you.” I imagine they whisper back, grateful.

One Tuesday I drive to the grocery store again, though we don’t need anything. I walk the aisles slowly, scanning faces. I’m not sure what I’m looking for—my younger self? The woman with the coupon? A portal to the Tuesday I lost? In the floral section, bouquets stand in buckets like refugees from somebody’s garden party. I buy daisies because they look stunned, like they can’t believe they’ve been severed from roots. At the register, the teenage cashier has been replaced by a machine that speaks in a placid automaton voice: “Please place your items in the bagging area.” I obey. The daisies tremble. I drive home the long way, past the elementary school where recess is a swirl of tiny screams, past the cemetery where the stones look like baby teeth waiting to be claimed. I roll down the window. The wind tastes of mulch and exhaust and something green trying to happen.

Back home I find Mark and the girl in the backyard planting a tree. It’s a skinny thing, more stick than canopy, tied to a stake for courage. They’re both wearing mismatched gloves, their knees dark with loam. I watch from the window, unseen. Mark says something I can’t hear; the girl throws back her head and laughs—sunlight catching in her throat like a swallowed star. I press my palm to the glass. Inside my chest, something cracks along an old fault line. Not a break, more like tectonic drift: continents reconfiguring. I carry the daisies outside. The three of us plant them around the baby tree, a perimeter of white faces nodding yes yes yes. Soil under my fingernails feels like a credential. When we finish, the girl drizzles water from a dinosaur-shaped watering can. Mark wraps his arm around my waist. I don’t reverse-engineer anything; I simply lean. Night seeps in charcoal around the edges. Somewhere an onion in the earth divides and doubles, cell by cell, patient as a secret. I close my eyes and time pools in me—no longer leaking but circulating: tide, breath, orbit. The tree shivers. Tomorrow it will be taller.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
